chapterTitle: Chapter 7: Semantic HTML, Forms & Structured Data

MCQs.question: Which semantic tag should wrap the primary, unique content of a page?
How many <main> elements can a page have?
Which tag represents self-contained, independently shareable content?
What is the purpose of the <label> tag in forms?

options.text: Associates text with an input for accessibility

MCQs.question: Which input type creates a dropdown calendar for selecting a date?
What attribute makes a form field mandatory?
Which table tag is used for header cells?
What is the <aside> tag used for?

options.text: The main content of the page

MCQs.question: Which HTML attribute links a <label> to its <input>?
Should HTML tables be used for page layout?

options.text: Yes, but only for simple layouts
No, tables are for data only; use CSS for layout

ShortQuestions.question: What is the difference between <section> and <article>? Give one example of each.
ShortQuestions.preferredAnswer:
**<section>** groups **thematically related content** that is part of a larger whole. Example: A "Our Services" section within a page.

**<article>** represents **self-contained, independent content** that could be shared on its own (e.g. a blog post, news item).

**Test:** If the content could stand alone as a blog post or product card → use `<article>`. If it's a themed group within a page → use `<section>`.

ShortQuestions.question: Why is the <label> tag important for form accessibility? How does the for attribute work?
ShortQuestions.preferredAnswer:
The `<label>` tag: (1) **Screen readers** read the label when the input is focused; (2) **Clicking the label** focuses/activates the input (easier for checkboxes/radio on mobile).

The `for` attribute matches the `id` of the input: `<label for="email">Email:</label>` and `<input type="email" id="email" name="email">`. The values must match exactly.

ShortQuestions.question: Write a complete HTML table showing 3 products with columns for Product Name, Price, and In Stock. Include <thead>, <tbody>, and <caption>.
Explain the difference between <header>, <main>, and <footer>. How do they work together on a page?
ShortQuestions.preferredAnswer:
**<header>** — Introductory content (logo, nav), typically same across all pages, at the top.
**<main>** — Primary, unique content that changes from page to page. Only ONE per page.
**<footer>** — Closing content (copyright, links), typically same across all pages, at the bottom.

Together: `<header>` (same) + `<main>` (unique for this page) + `<footer>` (same).

ShortQuestions.question: List 4 HTML5 form validation attributes and explain what each one does with an example.
ShortQuestions.preferredAnswer:
1. **required** — Field must be filled: `<input type="text" name="name" required>`
2. **minlength** — Minimum characters: `<input type="text" name="username" minlength="3">`
3. **max** — Maximum numeric value: `<input type="number" name="age" max="120">`
4. **pattern** — Must match regex: `<input type="text" name="zip" pattern="[0-9]{5}" title="Enter 5-digit zip code">`

These enable client-side validation without JavaScript.
